title: Tailoring the Approach
summary: The consolidated tailoring, mindset and situational-answer playbook: how to read the delivery model, choose and tailor the approach, apply the PMP decision principles, and match the best first action to the classic scenarios grouped by knowledge area.

sections.heading: What tailoring is and when to tailor
sections.body: Tailoring is the deliberate adaptation of the approach, governance and artifacts to fit the project's environment, complexity and objectives. The PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition keeps tailoring as a core concept - it is not one of the six named principles, but is realised by fitting the approach to the context rather than applying every process, document and control the same way on every effort. PMBOK 8 is principle- and outcome-based - 6 principles plus 7 performance domains (Governance; Scope; Schedule; Finance; Stakeholders; Resources; Risk) - while development approach and life cycle now sits in The Standard for Project Management, where tailoring shapes how each domain is applied. Its whole purpose is value delivery: keep what helps deliver value, drop what does not. On the exam, approach selection is rarely a definition question - it is embedded in a scenario that signals how the work should be organised, governed and delivered.

blocks.title: What tailoring can change
lines: Process: how much upfront planning, how changes are approved, how risks are reviewed, how decisions escalate.
Governance: review frequency, approval thresholds, reporting, compliance checkpoints.
Artifacts: schedule detail, WBS vs backlog as the scope artifact, lightweight vs formal documentation.

blocks.title: Tailoring is not skipping discipline
blocks.text: Good tailoring keeps what is necessary for value delivery, compliance and control while cutting unnecessary overhead. It applies the right amount of process for the situation - tailoring in action - and does not mean doing less for its own sake.

blocks.title: Exam tip
lines: Identify the implied delivery approach before you choose an answer.
A technically reasonable action can still be wrong if it fits the wrong life cycle.
PMI favours contextual fit over method loyalty - match the method to the work.

sections.heading: How to dissect any question
sections.body: Run this on every question before you read the options.

items.title: Read the LAST sentence first
lines: Is it asking first, next, best, or most important? The call word changes the answer.
First / next -> reward correct process order and the least-drastic correct action.
Best / most appropriate -> reward the option that fixes the root cause through proper process.

items.title: Classify the situation
lines: Predictive, agile or hybrid?
Planning, executing, monitoring and controlling, or closing?
A current problem (issue) or a future uncertainty (risk)?

items.title: Scan for keyword triggers
lines: Find the trigger phrase - it usually decides the answer on a close question.

items.title: Eliminate aggressively
lines: Strike options that bypass process (especially a baseline).
Strike options that jump straight to escalate or remove-someone.
Strike options where the PM decides for an agile team.
Strike passive options (just log / monitor / inform) when action is needed.

items.title: Choose the disciplined move
lines: Proactive, follows the correct process/order, and gives authority to the right role.

blocks.title: Separate risk from issue and check authority
lines: Already happened -> issue. Might happen -> risk.
Agile team decides how the work is done; the Product Owner prioritises value; the PM removes blockers.
Diagnose first, follow the agreed process, escalate only after the normal path fails.

sections.heading: Predictive vs Agile vs Hybrid - read the signals
sections.body: The delivery approach tells you the flavour of the right answer. Read the wording, then match the answer style.

groups.label: Signals
lines: Stable, well-understood scope.
Baseline, WBS, change request, CCB.
Critical path, earned value, formal sign-off.

groups.label: Right answer style
lines: Follow the formal process.
Submit a change request when a baseline changes.
Assess impact and protect the approved baseline.

groups.label: Signals
lines: Changing requirements.
Sprint, iteration, user story, backlog, velocity.
Product Owner, Scrum Master, standup, self-organising team.

groups.label: Right answer style
lines: Empower the team; diagnose WITH the team.
Let the Product Owner own priority.
Remove impediments; improve in retrospectives.

groups.label: Signals
lines: Tailoring is mentioned.
Predictive and agile terms appear together.
Some requirements fixed, some uncertain; stage gates plus iterations.

groups.label: Right answer style
lines: Govern the stable parts; iterate the uncertain parts.
Respect governance while enabling adaptation.
Break down and prioritise the KNOWN requirements rather than waiting.

rows.value: Agile.

rows.key: Baseline, WBS, CCB, critical path, earned value
rows.value: Predictive.
Hybrid.

rows.key: Part fixed by contract, part on a backlog
rows.value: Hybrid - preserve the contract boundary, iterate the rest.
Hybrid - keep approvals, support the ceremonies.

blocks.title: Authority check
lines: Predictive: the PM plans and directs.
Agile: the team self-organises; the Product Owner prioritises value; the servant leader removes impediments.
If the scenario says sprint / backlog / PO, do not jump to the CCB unless it clearly introduces hybrid governance.

sections.heading: Predictive, iterative, incremental and adaptive
sections.body: The PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition places these on a continuum in its development approach and life cycle guidance, now part of The Standard for Project Management; the team chooses what best fits the context and the value it needs to deliver. Many real projects combine the patterns.

rows.value:
Plan first. Scope, schedule and cost defined early and managed against baselines.
Fits stable, well-understood work with formal governance, compliance or fixed-scope contracts.

Refine repeatedly. Early versions improve through cycles of feedback.
Fits when the solution needs learning and refinement even if the vision is broadly understood.

Deliver in slices. Each increment adds usable capability.
Fits when the product divides into meaningful pieces and early partial value helps.

Learn fast. Change-driven, short cycles, close collaboration, frequent reprioritisation.
Fits high uncertainty, evolving needs and fast feedback.

blocks.title: What hybrid looks like
blocks.text: Hybrid deliberately mixes predictive and adaptive by part of the work. Governance, procurement, stage approvals and compliance stay predictive while solution development runs in iterations with a backlog and regular reviews. It fits when some requirements are fixed and others are expected to evolve.

blocks.title: Mnemonic
lines: Predictive = plan first.
Adaptive = learn fast.
Iterative = refine repeatedly.
Incremental = deliver in slices.

sections.heading: Factors that drive tailoring
sections.body: Choose the approach from context, not preference - this is tailoring applied to the development approach and life cycle. These drivers point you toward predictive, adaptive or hybrid, always in service of value delivery.

rows.value:
Stable and clear -> predictive.
Evolving or uncertain -> adaptive / hybrid.

High technical / market / solution uncertainty -> adaptive to learn fast.
Low, clear path -> predictive is efficient.

Available and engaged, quick decisions -> agile viable.
Infrequent, locked early -> predictive.
Favours predictive controls and traceability - but does not rule out agile; regulated shops often go hybrid.

Frequent small releases -> incremental / adaptive.
One tightly integrated delivery -> predictive.

lines: Senior buy-in?
Does the team trust one another?
Will the organisation let the team decide and accept an evolving plan?
Small enough and experienced enough?
Ready access to the customer?
Ready access to each other?
How often will requirements change?
Can the work be delivered incrementally?
How critical is the deliverable?

blocks.title: Reading the filters
lines: Strong across culture, team and project -> fully adaptive.
Weak across them -> predictive.
Mixed picture -> hybrid.
High change rate, incremental delivery, engaged customer and trusting culture point to adaptive.

sections.heading: The PMP decision principles
sections.body: Most ties break the same way: the proactive option that follows process and empowers the right role wins. These principles decide it.

items.title: Diagnose before treating
lines: Assess first - satisfaction survey, power/interest grid, variance / impact analysis - before jumping to a solution.

items.title: Follow the process
lines: Do not bypass formal change control or governance, especially around a baseline.

items.title: Least-drastic correct action
lines: Talk to the functional manager over escalating.
Remind of a ground rule over rewriting the process.
Diagnose with the team over deciding for them.

items.title: Value and people
lines: Prove value with a prototype / MVP.
Empower the team; support the Product Owner.
Address emotions first; never assign blame.

blocks.title: When in doubt - the mindset map

rows.value: Change request (not modify directly).
Issue Log (not Risk Register).
Risk Register (not Issue Log).
Log it in the register first, then act.
Diagnose WITH the team (not PM decides / change the timeline).
A one-on-one (not a team-wide fix).
Prototype / MVP (not extend the timeline).

rows.key: Resource pulled by a functional manager
rows.value: Talk to the functional manager first.
Register them first; update the Engagement Plan.
PMO (not the Sponsor).
Address EARLIER.
Collaborate / confront the root cause.

blocks.title: Eliminate fast
lines: Bypasses process - especially editing a baseline without a change request.
PM deciding for a self-organising agile team.
Jumps straight to escalate-to-sponsor or remove/replace the person.
Passive (just inform / monitor / log) when action is needed.
Treats a current, realised problem as a future risk.
Updates the wrong artifact (Register when it should be the Plan).
Quantifies before identifying, or ignores process order.
Assumes rewards when the real issue is support, skills or conflict.

sections.heading: The document traps: Plan vs Register, Issue vs Risk
sections.body: Distractors constantly swap the Plan and the Register, and treat a realised problem as a future risk. These are the single most repeated distinctions.

rows.value: HOW risk is run - methodology, roles, categories, thresholds. Made once. Review the approach/strategy -> the Plan.
The living list of risks, owners and responses. Log / analyse / update a specific risk -> the Register.
HOW you engage each stakeholder. Update when roles or strategies change.
WHO they are - names, roles, interest/influence. Update for a new person or detail.

blocks.title: Issue vs Risk - has it happened yet?
lines: Yes, it is happening now -> Issue Log.
No / uncertain, it might happen -> Risk Register.
Classic trap: treating a current, realised event (a defect that already appeared) as a future risk.

blocks.title: Updates free of change control
blocks.text: These update WITHOUT a change request: Stakeholder Register, Risk Register, Issue Log, Assumption Log, Lessons Learned Register. Any change to a scope / schedule / cost baseline needs a change request - even a free enhancement with no cost or schedule impact must be documented through change control.

sections.heading: Agile metrics, boards and ceremonies
sections.body: The exam tests agile mechanics, not just mindset.

rows.value: Historical throughput (story points per iteration). Use it to FORECAST capacity - do not add money or people to inflate it.
Size effort / complexity, not hours or location. More points = more work, wherever done.
Limit work-in-progress to improve flow; pull the next item only when a slot frees.
The TEAM's own progress visibility during the sprint.
Communicates progress to STAKEHOLDERS.
OKR sets an Objective plus measurable Key Results (the goal). A KPI is an ongoing indicator tracked against a target (how you track).

lines: Improve the PROCESS so a problem cannot recur; lift motivation for next iterations.
Team-wide friction or dropped morale -> the retro, not a one-on-one.
Missed or oversized functionality -> write detailed user stories (INVEST) and refine iteratively.
Do not fall back on heavyweight requirements documents.
Pull them into EARLIER iterations to burn risk down while there is time to react.
Raised in a standup or review -> take it to the Product Owner, who owns backlog and product decisions.
Do not let the team vote scope up or down.

blocks.title: Agile tip
lines: Velocity forecasts capacity - it is not a target to inflate.
Team progress -> task board; stakeholder progress -> backlog / burndown.
Prevent recurrence -> retrospective; product / priority decision -> the Product Owner.

sections.heading: Stakeholders and communications
sections.body: Situation -> best first action. Register holds the who; the Engagement Plan holds the how.

items.title: Stakeholders uninformed at a review / meeting

groups.label: Best action
lines: Review the Communications Management Plan first - check what it already says before assuming new analysis.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Update the Stakeholder Register, then re-check the engagement approach for the new person.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Identify, analyse and add them to the Register FIRST.

groups.label: Trap
lines: Do not action their request or raise a change request before they are registered.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Elicit and negotiate requirements, weighting by influence - never at the cost of the approved objectives.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Explain the value (less rework) before negotiating reduced attendance.

items.title: Ad-hoc requests pile up / PM is bypassed

groups.label: Best action
lines: The communications plan has a gap - meet them to understand the need and UPDATE the plan.

groups.label: Trap
lines: Do not just resend the old plan or force a single channel.

items.title: Report in the wrong format for the CEO

groups.label: Best action
lines: Use interactive (two-way) communication to confirm understanding - confirmation beats one-way instruction.

rows.value: Maps current (C) vs desired (D) engagement: Unaware -> Resistant -> Neutral -> Supportive -> Leading. Use when you do not know the gap; monitor over time.
High/high = Manage Closely; high/low = Keep Satisfied; low/high = Keep Informed; low/low = Monitor.
Power, Legitimacy, Urgency. All three = Definitive, top priority.
Interactive (meetings/calls, best for complex); push (emails/reports); pull (portals/wikis).

blocks.title: Stakeholder tip
lines: Engagement is tailored per stakeholder - that tailoring lives in the PLAN, not the register.
Lead decision-makers with business VALUE, not technical detail.
Stakeholders not attending -> reach out individually to understand why.

sections.heading: Resources and the functional manager
sections.body: A functional manager reassigning your people is a resource conflict, not an automatic escalation.

groups.label: Step 1
lines: Negotiate directly with the functional manager to protect the commitment.

groups.label: Step 2
lines: Escalate to senior management / sponsor only if collaboration fails and the project is genuinely harmed.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Absorb it with proactive scheduling - resource leveling / smoothing - rather than just logging the issue.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Recover, negotiate for, or upskill the people you already have.

groups.label: Trap
lines: Reaching to create something new (team, tool, process) when you should develop existing resources.

rows.value: Little / no authority; the functional manager runs the show.
Coordinator / expediter; limited authority.
Authority shared with the functional manager.
Moderate-to-high; resources still report functionally.
High / full authority; the team reports to the PM.

blocks.title: Resource tip
lines: Talk to the functional manager FIRST; escalate only when negotiation fails.
More matrix / functional wording -> the PM negotiates for people, not directs them.
Recover / negotiate / develop before you recreate.

sections.heading: Risk
sections.body: Respect the order: identify first, respond second. Every response gets an owner.

items.title: Plan Risk Management
lines: Defines HOW risk will be managed (the strategy).

items.title: Identify Risks
lines: Create the register (high-level first); performed throughout.

items.title: Qualitative analysis
lines: Prioritise by probability x impact.

items.title: Quantitative analysis
lines: Model the numeric effect (not always done).

items.title: Plan Risk Responses
lines: Develop response actions - the risk action plan.

items.title: Implement Risk Responses
lines: Owners carry them out.

items.title: Monitor Risks
lines: Track residual/secondary risks, watch for new ones, confirm responses worked.

groups.label: Best action
lines: DOCUMENT it in the register first, then analyse and respond.

groups.label: Trap
lines: Quantifying or solving before it is even identified.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Consult the risk response plan / contract SOW and apply the planned response.

groups.label: Trap
lines: Improvising a new response.

items.title: Known risk occurs, you use the planned response

groups.label: Best action
lines: Update the register with the new status.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Update the risk management plan and implement the new response.

groups.label: Best action
lines: ASSESS it against the plan before revising the schedule - do not jump to exploit.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Escalate.

items.text: Model variability in a value (durations, costs, quantities) as a probability distribution; quantitative.
Facilitated, anonymous, iterative polling to consensus for risk identification with a dispersed team.
Examines whether the risk PROCESS and responses are effective, not a single risk.
Probability x Impact; sum identified risks to size contingency.
For KNOWN risks; inside the baseline; the PM controls it.
For UNKNOWN risks; outside the baseline; needs approval to release.
A new risk created BY a response you implemented.
The risk that REMAINS after a response.

rows.value: Escalate, Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept.
Escalate, Exploit, Share, Enhance, Accept.
Active (set a contingency reserve) or passive (document, do nothing unless it fires). The only strategy shared by both.
Mitigate = reduce probability/impact (redundancy, QA). Transfer = shift impact AND ownership to a third party (insurance, contract).

blocks.title: Risk tip
lines: New risk -> log it in the register first, never jump to a number or solution.
Planned-for risk fires -> consult the response plan, do not improvise.
Every response gets a risk owner who implements and monitors it.
Minimise and Ignore are NOT real strategies - they are distractors for Mitigate and passive Accept.

sections.heading: Change control and scope
sections.body: Once scope, schedule or cost is baselined you cannot edit it directly - it flows through Perform Integrated Change Control.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Raise a change request, assess impact across constraints, the CCB decides, then update.

groups.label: Trap
lines: Modifying a baselined document directly because the change seems small or obvious.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Bring in expert judgment (SMEs, the CCB) to assess impact before committing - do not proceed blindly either.

items.title: Change request delay will cause bigger problems

groups.label: Best action
lines: Revise the change request so it reflects the full, current impact before it is evaluated.

items.title: Features dropped in negotiation now cause problems

groups.label: Best action
lines: Work WITH the client to negotiate including the missing features; resolve at client level before escalating.

groups.label: Best action
lines: The gap was in scope definition / the scope management plan; review Validate Scope for root cause.

rows.value: High-level scope and success criteria; starting point for the WBS. Approve before detailed planning. Business case and SOW feed it.
Built WITH the team by decomposing approved scope into work packages; deliverable-oriented, 100% rule; an internal tool.
Corrective action, preventive action, defect repair, updates.
Customer requirement = general, in their words. Technical requirement = specific and testable; you translate one to the other.
Requirements Traceability Matrix maps each requirement to its test case(s); hand it to a test vendor with the test plan.

blocks.title: Change control tip
lines: Update / revise a baseline -> change request, almost every time.
Logs and registers are fair game to update without one.
Even a free enhancement with no cost/schedule impact still goes through change control and is documented.

sections.heading: Agile and hybrid practice
sections.body: Diagnose WITH the team, empower the Product Owner on priority, improve the process in the retro.

items.title: Introduce story points to a Kanban team

groups.label: Best action
lines: Estimate the NEW backlog items only first; do not stop to re-point work in progress.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Establish a centralised repository for documentation and validation to improve knowledge sharing.

groups.label: Trap
lines: Assuming training is the fix.

items.title: Team member keeps picking easy stories

groups.label: Best action
lines: A private conversation to understand the root cause before assuming a skills gap.

items.title: Users uneasy with last iteration's features

groups.label: Best action
lines: Obtain stakeholder feedback and team insights before acting.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Bring them into sprint / iteration or release planning to re-prioritise - not formal change control.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Acknowledge the frustration first, then explain the value each ceremony provides (servant leadership).

items.title: Team has productivity ideas in a transition

groups.label: Best action
lines: Capture and act on them in retrospectives - the primary forum for process improvement.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Build a prototype / proof of concept / MVP - a usable slice early. Show, do not tell.

blocks.title: Agile / hybrid tip
lines: Self-organising teams decide HOW to do the work - the servant leader lets them diagnose and solve.
Do not unilaterally change the timeline or scope, and do not skip backlog refinement.
Sequence risk early; support the Product Owner rather than override product decisions.

sections.heading: Governance, initiation and handover
sections.body: Match the level of the problem to the level of the authority.

groups.label: Lesson
lines: The original PM should have documented assumptions in the charter and identified high-level risks early. Strong early planning prevents most later pain.

items.title: Client PM lacks financial authority, causing delays

groups.label: Best action
lines: Escalate to the steering committee - authority and governance gaps belong at the governance level.

items.title: Team leader bypasses the PM to the sponsor

groups.label: Step 1
lines: Remind them of the existing communication ground rule directly.

groups.label: Step 2
lines: Only if it recurs, strengthen the agreement to require PM approval.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Check the lessons-learned database and other OPAs before anything else.

items.title: Stakeholder wants to pause to assess a new process

groups.label: Best action
lines: Consult the sponsor - strategic direction changes are the sponsor's call.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Identify high-level risks first, then get the sponsor and key stakeholders explicitly aligned before proceeding.

blocks.title: Governance tip
lines: Address a ground-rule violation directly before changing the process.
Strategic pause / direction -> sponsor. Authority gap -> steering committee.
Organisation-wide alignment across projects -> PMO, not the sponsor.

sections.heading: Cost, schedule and estimating
sections.body: Assess impact before mitigating; ask the right source for an estimate.

items.title: Status report on an over-budget project

groups.label: Best action
lines: Compare actual results to the plan (variance analysis); focus on current performance data.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Assess economic feasibility with a cost-benefit analysis (BCR). Below 1, costs outweigh benefits and you consult the sponsor.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Analyse the business impact and reprioritise deliverables before choosing a mitigation.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Be proactive: develop a bottom-up estimate of the remaining work.

groups.label: Trap
lines: Reaching first for contingency, parallelism or a change request.

groups.label: Ongoing work
lines: Ask the people doing the work.

groups.label: New / specialised area
lines: Ask domain / subject-matter experts - not the sponsor or procurement.

items.title: Sponsor wants an early view of resource needs

groups.label: Best action
lines: Share the Resource Breakdown Structure (RBS), not the WBS or SOW.

rows.value: Top-down from a similar past project; fast, cheap, least accurate.
Rate x quantity; accurate with good data.
Sum the detailed work packages; most accurate, most effort.
tE = (O + 4M + P) / 6; range approx (P - O) / 6.
The LONGEST path; zero float; drives duration. Two independent paths -> the critical one is the longer.
Total float = slip without delaying the project; free float = without delaying the successor. Critical-path activities have zero total float.
Add resources to the critical path; costs money.
Run sequential work in parallel; adds risk / rework.
Leveling can extend the schedule; smoothing stays within float.

blocks.title: Cost / schedule tip
lines: Status report -> variance analysis. Continue-or-not -> cost-benefit.
Crash = money, fast-track = risk; only the critical path shortens the project.
Never cut planned tests just to gain float - that trades quality for time.

sections.heading: Team and leadership
sections.body: Diagnose before treating: survey the team for a whole-team issue, hold a one-on-one for an individual one.

groups.label: Best action
lines: A private one-on-one to find the root cause.

groups.label: Trap
lines: Reassigning work or telling the whole team to focus more.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Run a satisfaction evaluation / confidential poll for the root cause before team-building or incentives.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Acknowledge and address the emotions FIRST (emotional intelligence), then problem-solve. Never assign blame.

groups.label: Best action
lines: You (the servant leader) reinforce it with the team; address a repeat offender privately. Not HR's or the sponsor's job.

items.title: New members struggling under a tight timeline

groups.label: Best action
lines: Pair them with experienced members via job shadowing / peer mentoring - faster than formal training under pressure.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Build cultural intelligence across the WHOLE team; revisit the team charter together.

groups.label: Trap
lines: Adapting only one way or focusing on one site's customs.

items.title: Serious slippage the team cannot absorb

groups.label: Best action
lines: Work up a revised plan with the team, then take it to the sponsor. A small blocker -> just remove it, do not escalate.

rows.value: Serves the team, removes impediments. The agile default and the move when shifting predictive -> hybrid.
Task / results focused; tells the team what and how. For crises, inexperienced teams, when structure is needed.
Match style to team maturity: Directing -> Coaching -> Supporting -> Delegating.
Motivates through rewards and penalties.
Inspires through vision and growth.
Hands-off; suits a highly capable, self-directed team.
Hierarchy of needs; lower needs first.
Hygiene factors only PREVENT dissatisfaction; motivators drive satisfaction.
Theory X (need control) vs Theory Y (self-motivated).
Achievement, Affiliation, Power.
Effort -> performance -> a reward the person actually values.
Long-term employment and loyalty drive productivity.

items.title: Collaborate / problem-solve
lines: Confront the issue together for win/win. The PMP-preferred mode.

items.title: Compromise
lines: Both give something up (lose/lose).

items.title: Smooth / accommodate
lines: Emphasise agreement; may not fix the cause.

items.title: Force / direct
lines: Impose a solution (win/lose); emergencies only.

items.title: Withdraw / avoid
lines: Retreat; trivial issues only.
Tuckman stages: Forming -> Storming -> Norming -> Performing -> Adjourning.

blocks.title: People tip
lines: Individual issue -> one-on-one; team issue -> the team.
Emotions -> address them first. Conflict -> collaborate / confront the root cause.
Effort -> a valued reward = Expectancy. Manager owns process; leader sets vision.

sections.heading: Procurement and closure
sections.body: Verify against acceptance criteria before acting on a closing defect; project money cannot fund operations.

items.title: Contracts no longer align with objectives

groups.label: Best action
lines: Recommend terminating the misaligned ones through the proper channels.

items.title: Client wants to spend leftover budget on ops

groups.label: Best action
lines: Reject the request and close the project financials - reserves and project funds do not cover post-project operations.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Review the deliverables against the acceptance criteria first; a non-accepted deliverable -> change request (defect repair) to the CCB.

groups.label: Best action
lines: Formal handover of accountability plus training and documentation for users.

items.title: Accept deliverables
lines: Formal sign-off first; it is the sponsor's, not the functional managers'.

items.title: Close procurement
lines: Verify work and settle contracts; do not close until finance confirms payment.

items.title: Lessons learned
lines: Analyse WITH stakeholders, then store in the register (an OPA).

items.title: Release the team
lines: Last - and only after finance confirms.

rows.value: RFI gather info; RFQ price a well-defined buy; RFP complex / unknown solution - most formal, and a new vendor needs it.
Seller carries most risk (FFP, FPIF, FPEPA for long / international deals with inflation or currency risk).
Buyer carries most risk (CPFF, CPIF, CPAF).
Medium risk; staff augmentation or small / uncertain scope.
Award (CPAF) = subjective reward; incentive (CPIF / FPIF) = objective formula against targets.
Plan (make-or-buy) -> Conduct (RFx, select, award) -> Control (monitor vendor) -> Close (verify, pay).
Negotiation -> arbitration -> litigation (last resort).

blocks.title: Procurement / closure tip
lines: Complex need / unknown solution -> RFP; well-defined scope -> Fixed Price.
Monitoring a vendor = Control Procurements; paying them off = Close Procurements.
Closure order: accept -> close contracts -> lessons learned -> release the team.

sections.heading: Quality processes and tools
sections.body: Quality is built in and continuously improved, not inspected in. Do not cut a healthy QA budget just because quality looks fine.

items.title: Plan Quality
lines: Decide the standards and how you will meet them (early).

items.title: Manage Quality
lines: Quality assurance - right PROCESSES, audits, prevention, improvement. A quality audit = Manage Quality.

items.title: Control Quality
lines: Inspect the DELIVERABLES for defects; statistical sampling is a tool here.

items.title: Validate Scope
lines: The CUSTOMER formally accepts (after Control Quality, internally).

blocks.title: The seven quality tools - which to reach for

rows.value: Cause-and-effect / fishbone / Ishikawa (one tool, three names).
Pareto chart.
Histogram.

rows.key: Is the process stable / in control?
rows.value: Control chart.
Scatter diagram.
Checklist (check sheet).

rows.key: Map process steps / trend over time
rows.value: Flowchart / run chart.

blocks.title: Cost of quality and limits
lines: Conformance = prevention + appraisal; nonconformance = internal + external failure. Prevention is cheaper than failure.
Control limits are the process's own voice (set by the process); specification limits come from the customer.
Accuracy = close to the true value; precision = tightly clustered repeats. A process can be precise yet inaccurate.
Test a small improvement then standardise it -> Deming PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act).

blocks.title: Quality tip
lines: Audit / process focus = Manage Quality.
Inspect deliverables for defects = Control Quality.
Customer signs off = Validate Scope.

sections.heading: Roles, change models and the formula bank
sections.body: The quick-reference layer behind the situational patterns.

rows.value: Delivers day to day; in agile, facilitates and removes impediments.
Funds and champions ONE project, owns the business case, removes high-level obstacles, signs off acceptance.
Sets standards / governance across projects; a key decision-maker for alignment; can authorise or terminate projects.
Owns the product, the backlog and its priority, and what gets released.
Owns the people / resources in a matrix org.
Individual: Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement - each person, in order.
Individual: Ending -> Neutral Zone -> New Beginning; the transition lags the external change.
Individual emotional curve: denial -> anger -> bargaining -> depression -> acceptance.
System: unfreeze -> change -> refreeze. The simplest frame.
System: top-led 8-step organisational sequence.
System: a group's performance dips (chaos) then recovers; normalises the dip.

blocks.title: Formula bank
blocks.note: Variance = EV minus (PV or AC); index = EV over (PV or AC); below 1 / negative is bad.

lines: Cost variance; negative = over budget.
Schedule variance; negative = behind.
Cost efficiency; below 1 = poor.
Schedule efficiency; below 1 = slower.
Estimate at completion when current performance continues.
Variance at completion; negative = expected overrun.
PERT; most-likely weighted by 4. Std dev = (P - O) / 6.
Size contingency from identified risks.
Above 1 = benefits win; below 1 = costs win.
Communication paths grow fast as people are added.

blocks.title: Exam-day reminders
lines: Read the last sentence first; first / next -> process order and the least-drastic correct action.
Agile / hybrid -> empower, diagnose with the team, support the Product Owner. Predictive -> formal process and change requests.
Eliminate answers that are passive, skip process, or put the wrong person in charge.
Trust the PMP mindset: proactive, servant-leader, value-driven, process-respecting.

source: Consolidated from prior PM Cram cheat sheets
